<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Takaosan Kofun]][[ja:高尾山古墳]][[zh:高尾山古墳]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Takaosan Kofun]][[ja:高尾山古墳]][[zh:高尾山古墳]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高尾山古墳]][[zh:高尾山古墳]]









{{Expand Japanese}}
{{Otheruses||Kofun on Mount Takao in Kashiwara City, Osaka Prefecture (Takaozan Site)|Takaozan (Osaka Prefecture)}}
{{Infobox ancient site
|name = Takaozan Kofun
|image = File:Takaosan-kofun (Numazu) zenkei.JPG
|location = {{ill|Numazu|WD=Q241037|1=Numazu}}
|coordinates = {{Coord|35.122981|N|138.861172|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|omitted = JP-22
|type = [[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]]
|area = Kofun length: 62mHeight: 4.7m (estimated at approximately 5m)
|burial = [[Wooden Coffin|Wooden coffin]] direct burial
|built = Around the year {{ill|250|en|250|WD=Q31967|lt=250|1=250}}
|buried_person =
|burial goods = Bronze mirror (animal belt mirror of the embossed style from the Shangfang Workshop) 1 piece, magatama 1 piece, spear 1 piece, 32 iron arrowheads, 1 spearhead-plane, and a large amount of earthenware from the tumulus and the surrounding ditch.
|Historic site = {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site|1=Historic site}} Historic site by the national government ("Takaozan Kofun")
|notable_features =
|map = Japan Shizuoka
|type = Zenpō-kōhō-fun - South
|architectural_styles = {{ill|Zenpō-kōhō-fun|WD=Q11397225|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun}}
}}
{{osm box|n|4735442868}}

'''Takaozan Kofun''' (Takaozan Kofun) is a [[kofun]] located in [[Higashikumando]] ("East Kumando"), [[Numazu City]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It has the type of a [[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped mound with a rectangular rear and trapezoidal front)Numazu City Board of Education (2012) summary report. It has been Historic site as a {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site|1=Historic site}} by the national government.[https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/94070501.html Cultural Council's Recommendation (Designation of Historic sites, Places of Scenic Beauty, and Natural Monuments)] (Agency for Cultural Affairs press release, June 24, 2024).Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology, Notification No. 142, October pp, 2024.

The Numazu City Board of Education estimates its construction to have been around the year {{ill|250|en|250|WD=Q31967|lt=250|1=250}}, during the early [[Kofun period]] (the period of the [[:en:Yamatai Kingdom|Yamatai Kingdom]]), making it one of the oldest and largest kofun in Eastern Japan from the early appearance of such Kofuns.[https://www.city.numazu.shizuoka.jp/shisei/profile/bunkazai/kofun/takaosan.htm Numazu City, 2024, "Takaozan Kofun"] Accessed October 20, 2024. The {{ill|Japanese Archaeological Association|en|Japanese Archaeological Association|WD=Q6158105|lt=Japanese Archaeological Association|1=Japanese Archaeological Association}} has evaluated it as an extremely important site for understanding the process of typeing the early statehood marked by the kofun culture.[http://archaeology.jp/maibun/nikokyo2015-12.htm Japanese Archaeological Association, 2015, "Statement by the President Calling for Preservation of the Takaozan Kofun in Numazu City, Shizuoka Prefecture"] Accessed July 8, 2017.

Additionally, the site lies in the path of the planned construction of the urban arterial road (Numazu South Isshiki Line), which Numazu City considers a critical route for the urban structure.[https://www.city.numazu.shizuoka.jp/shisei/takaosan/jyoukyou.htm Numazu City, "Status of Urban Arterial Road built for Numazu South Isshiki Line"] Accessed July 8, 2017. Consequently, debates regarding the coexistence of road construction and kofun preservation have taken place, with various measures being examined to achieve both.[https://www.city.numazu.shizuoka.jp/shisei/g-shigiki/g-sigiki/dayori/h27/pdf/207-10.pdf Numazu City, 2015, "Submission of a Petition Regarding the Continuation of the Takaozan Kofun" (PDF file)] Accessed July 8, 2017. [https://www.city.numazu.shizuoka.jp/shisei/takaosan/index.htm Numazu City, 2017, "Preservation of the Takaozan Kofun and Road Development"] Accessed July 8, 2017.

== location and Environment of the Kofun ==
[[File:Ashitaka Volcano Relief map, SRTM-1 and Takkaosan kofun.jpg|thumb|300px|map of {{ill|Mount Ashitaka|en|Mount Ashitaka|WD=Q3247941|lt=Ashitaka Volcano|1=Mount Ashitaka}} and its surroundings. The red circle indicates the location of Takaozan Kofun. The lowland area to the southeast, marked in white, corresponds to the typeer {{ill|Ukishima Marsh|ja|浮島沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Marsh|1=Ukishima numa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558521}}.]]
The {{ill|Takaozan Kofun|ja|高尾山古墳|1=Takaozan Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|qq=}} is located at the southernmost ridge extending from {{ill|Mount Ashitaka|en|Mount Ashitaka|WD=Q3247941|lt=Ashitaka Volcano|1=Mount Ashitaka}}. The elevation at the site of the kofun is approximately 20 meters. While situated on a ridge, it cannot be regarded as a Kofun constructed on elevated terrain.<ref>Numazu City Board of Education (2012), p.1; Sato (2013a), p.pp</ref>

{{ill|Mount Ashitaka|en|Mount Ashitaka|WD=Q3247941|lt=Ashitaka Volcano|1=Mount Ashitaka}} is believed to have begun activity around 400,000 years ago. From about 170,000 years ago, it actively emitted {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=andesite|1=Andesite}} and {{ill|Dacite|en|Dacite|WD=Q466481|lt=dacite|1=Dacite}} lava flows towards its southeastern flank, typeing the gentle hills now seen in the areas of modern-day {{ill|Numazu|en|Numazu|WD=Q241037|lt=Numazu|1=Numazu}} City and {{ill|Nagaizumi|en|Nagaizumi|WD=Q1204459|lt=Nagaizumi|1=Nagaizumi}} Town. {{ill|Takaozan Kofun|ja|高尾山古墳|1=Takaozan Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|qq=}} was constructed at the edge of these hills, shaped by volcanic activity that began roughly 170,000 years ago.<ref>Numazu City Board of Education (2005), pp.36; Numazu City Board of Education (2012), pp.1-4</ref>

The volcanic activity of {{ill|Mount Ashitaka|en|Mount Ashitaka|WD=Q3247941|lt=Ashitaka Volcano|1=Mount Ashitaka}} ended about 100,000 years ago. Subsequently, the nearby {{ill|Ancient Mount Fuji|ja|古富士火山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Mount Fuji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} became active, depositing large amounts of volcanic ash on the lower slopes of Ashitaka. This ash typeed thick {{ill|loam|ja|ローム層|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/loam|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ローム (土壌)|1=Loam|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1131303}} layers. During the latter period of Ancient Mount Fuji’s activity, an upper loam layer developed, characterized by alternating {{ill|Scoria|en|Scoria|WD=Q6642389|lt=scoria|1=Scoria}} layers and black bands of humus soil. During this time, volcanic deposits from the {{ill|Aira Caldera|en|Aira Caldera|WD=Q1198576|lt=Aira Caldera|1=Aira Caldera}}'s massive eruption, about 25,000 years ago, also accumulated in the Ashitaka foothills, mixing with deposits from Ancient Mount Fuji to create a stratum known as pseudo-loam.<ref>Numazu City Board of Education (2005), pp.37, 67; Numazu City Board of Education (2012), p.4</ref>

After Ancient Mount Fuji became dormant, the activity of the {{ill|New Fuji Volcano|ja|新期富士火山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Fuji Volcano|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} began around pp,000 years ago. Initially, the volcanic activity was dominated by large lava flows, which were obstructed by {{ill|Mount Ashitaka|en|Mount Ashitaka|WD=Q3247941|lt=Ashitaka Volcano|1=Mount Ashitaka}} and did not reach the area around {{ill|Takaozan Kofun|ja|高尾山古墳|1=Takaozan Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|qq=}}. As a result, black soil layers developed in the Ashitaka foothills, typeing the uppermost part of the loam layers. By the late {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period|1=Jōmon period}}, approximately 3,200 years ago, significant pumice eruptions occurred at {{ill|Kawago-daira|ja|カワゴ平|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawago-daira|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, part of the {{ill|Izu-Tobu|en|Izu-Tobu|WD=Q6102206|lt=Izu East Volcano Group|1=Izu-Tobu}}. However, these eruptions did not leave significant layers in the Ashitaka region. During the same period, large-map_size {{ill|Sector collapse|en|Sector collapse|WD=Q17992244|lt=mountain collapses|1=Sector collapse}} occurred on the eastern slopes of Mount Fuji, creating massive debris flows that accumulated in the {{ill|Kise River|ja|黄瀬川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kise River|1=Kise River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11677792}} basin. These events typeed the {{ill|Kise River alluvial fan|ja|黄瀬川扇状地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kise River alluvial fan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, transtypeing the eastern parts of modern-day {{ill|Numazu|en|Numazu|WD=Q241037|lt=Numazu|1=Numazu}} City from wetlands into dry land by the start of the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}}.<ref>Numazu City Board of Education (2005), p.38; Numazu City Board of Education (2012), pp.1-4</ref>

On the other hand, during the {{ill|Holocene glacial retreat|en|Holocene glacial retreat|WD=Q834405|lt=Jōmon transgression|1=Holocene glacial retreat}}, the sea reached the foothills of {{ill|Mount Ashitaka|en|Mount Ashitaka|WD=Q3247941|lt=Ashitaka Volcano|1=Mount Ashitaka}}. Subsequently, as the sea receded, sandbars typeed along the {{ill|Kano River|en|Kano River|WD=Q6364188|lt=Kano River|1=Kano River}}’s estuary, creating a water body between the Ashitaka foothills and the sandbars. This became the prototype for {{ill|Ukishima Marsh|ja|浮島沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Marsh|1=Ukishima numa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11558521}}. Over time, this water body transitioned from a brackish lagoon to a marshland and then to a wetland. During the construction of {{ill|Takaozan Kofun|ja|高尾山古墳|1=Takaozan Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|qq=}}, boats could navigate to within 2–3 kilometers southwest of the kofun.<ref>Numazu City Board of Education (2005), p.1; Numazu City Historic site Center (2016), p.3</ref>

Finally, from the very end of the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} to the early {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, new scoria layers were deposited on the Ashitaka foothills, including the area around {{ill|Takaozan Kofun|ja|高尾山古墳|1=Takaozan Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|qq=}}. The rear mound of {{ill|Takaozan Kofun|ja|高尾山古墳|1=Takaozan Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaozan Kofun|qq=}} was constructed directly atop these new scoria layers.<ref>Numazu City Board of Education (2005), pp.4, 15</ref>

== location and Environment of the Kofun ==
[[File:Ashitaka Volcano Relief map, SRTM-1 and Takaozan kofun.jpg|thumb|300px|Relief map of Mount Ashitaka and surrounding areas. The red circle marks Takaozan Kofun's location, while the southeast area shaded white represents the typeer Ukishima Marsh.]]
Takaozan Kofun is situated at the terminus of a ridge

'

== References ==
<references />

{{Kofun navbar}}

{{translated page|ja|高尾山古墳|version=103216137|comment=Imported full ja history}}



[[ja:高尾山古墳]]

<!--Takaozan Kofun--><!--Takaozan Kofun--><!--Takaozan Kofun--><!--Takaozan Kofun--><!--Takaozan Kofun--><!--Takaozan Kofun-->
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
{{Commons category|Takaosan Kofun}}
[[zh:高尾山古墳]]
{{wikidata link|Q11638291}}
[[Category:History of Numazu]]
[[Category:Kofun of Shizuoka prefecture]]
[[Category:National historic sites in Shizuoka prefecture]]
[[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
[[Category:Zenpō-kōhō-fun]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
